subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Logical section order with clear headings and appropriate ESS terminology throughout.
Major arguments flow and reader can follow the overall narrative structure.
weaknesses: Organisation falters in places (split tables, poorly referenced images).
Writing is verbose and contains minor grammatical and referencing errors.
Formatting inconsistencies and abrupt sentence breaks disrupt coherence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and organisation, terminology use and overall logic align with band 2. While the report is generally coherent and uses correct terminology, sustained clarity and concision are lacking, and formatting issues interrupt the readerâs flow, capping Communication at 2 marks.

strengths: The research question is sharply focused, clearly defining variables, units and experimental scope.
A thorough multi-paragraph background contextualises the Gulf hypoxic zone with numerical thresholds and authoritative sources.
Explicit, comprehensive explanation links the environmental issue to the investigationâs design.
weaknesses: Minor informality in phrasing (âto what extentâ) introduces slight ambiguity.
Economic and social dimensions of hypoxia are not addressed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands sit convincingly in the 5â6 band and only small editorial improvements were suggested. The question is coherent and focused, the issue context is detailed and justified, and the connections are comprehensively explained. The minor wording and absence of economic/social discussion do not detract from an otherwise exemplary response, meriting the top mark.

strengths: Well-detailed, repeatable method with clear apparatus lists and a pilot-studyâinformed change to immersion heating.
Explicit sequential procedure enhances reproducibility over the 20-day period.
Key risks (electrical shock, glass handling, plant disposal) are identified with mitigation measures described.
weaknesses: Sampling strategy lacks replication and justification for single daily readings.
No formal sampling rationale or discussion of sample-size adequacy.
Risk assessment omits broader environmental disposal protocols and a structured matrix.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The method design is appropriate and repeatable (band 4), risk considerations are competently described (band 3), but the sampling strategy is only outlined with significant omissions (band 2). These combine to place Planning solidly in the 3â4 band, earning 4 marks.

strengths: Evaluates how findings relate to Gulf hypoxia and acknowledges model-limitation in attributing only temperature effects.
Identifies strengths (e.g., use of native plants, experiment duration) and weaknesses (open-system design, light variability).
Suggests meaningful modifications and further research (adding trophic levels, nutrient factors) that address major model weaknesses.
weaknesses: Ecological significance of results is not quantitatively assessed.
Some methodological limitations (statistical replication, calibration drift) are omitted.
Improvements lack detailed implementation strategies and expected outcome metrics.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation of conclusions and methods is competent (band 3â4) and proposed improvements are meaningful. The discussion shows depth but omits quantitative assessment and fuller limitation coverage. This warrants 4 marks in Discussion and Evaluation.

strengths: All relevant raw data are tabulated and a clear, well-labelled scatter graph with trendlines presents every point.
Correct identification of a negative correlation and discussion of curve shape and RÂ² in the narrative.
Axes scaling, symbol differentiation and uncertainty annotations enhance clarity.
weaknesses: Analysis lacks quantitative metrics (e.g., explicit RÂ² values, correlation coefficients) and error propagation.
Conclusion is valid but supported by minimal numerical evidence and no comparative rate calculations.
Attribution of discrepancies overlooks biological oxygen demand and probe placement variability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation meets the 5â6 band, while data analysis and conclusion drawing align with the 3â4 band. Overall performance falls in the upper middle, justifying a score of 4 for Results, Analysis and Conclusion.

strengths: Offers a relevant application (agricultural runoff regulation, GHG reduction) linked to temperatureâs role in hypoxia.
Identifies some strengths and weaknesses of the solution in environmental terms.
weaknesses: Justification does not explicitly draw on the studyâs numerical findings or DO decline metrics.
Evaluation of solution lacks depth on feasibility, cost, stakeholder impacts or policy thresholds.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands fall solidly in band 2: one application is described with weak justification, and only some relevant strengths and weaknesses are evaluated. Further linkage to numerical results and deeper feasibility analysis would be needed for higher credit.

strandSelections.reasoning: ESS terminology (eutrophication, BOD, hypoxic) is used correctly, but wording is occasionally verbose (2000+ words) and includes minor grammatical slips, satisfying one criterion (terminology) more than the other (concision).  This aligns with band 2.

The student evaluates how well the findings relate to Gulf hypoxia, acknowledging that temperature is only one driver and discussing limitations of the model.  Nevertheless, the ecological significance is not quantified and alternative interpretations are not explored in depth, placing the work solidly in the 3â4 band with a score of 3.

The conclusion states that temperature affects DO and cites the steeper decline in the 31.5 Â°C tank.  This is consistent with the plotted data, but supporting numerical evidence is minimal and confidence levels are not addressed.  Therefore the conclusion is valid yet only partially supported, fitting the 3â4 band at the lower end.

The student comments on slopes, curvature, and RÂ² values, identifying a negative correlation.  However, no quantitative test (e.g., correlation coefficient, rate calculation, error propagation) is performed, and uncertainties are only mentioned qualitatively.  Analysis is thus correct but incomplete, aligned with the mid band (3â4) at a score of 3.

Proposed improvements include adding eutrophication factors, using correct plant ratios and introducing more trophic levels.  These address major weaknesses, but specifics on how to implement them or expected benefits are brief, so the response is meaningful yet not âsignificantâ per the top descriptor, earning 3.

A step-by-step procedure, apparatus list and variable table make the method repeatable, and daily DO measurements over 20 days should yield sufficient data for correlation analysis.  Nevertheless, the design lacks replication (only one warm and one cool tank), no control for light fluctuation is built in, and no statistical test is planned.  These shortcomings reduce confidence that the method alone can provide âsufficientâ data for robust conclusions, so the upper-middle band (4) is most appropriate.

Links between the Gulf hypoxic issue and the investigation are explicitly explained: warmer water holds less oxygen; therefore temperature change is modelled in two aquaria to mirror summer/winter conditions.  The student justifies chosen temperatures with sea-temperature data and refers back to hypoxic thresholds.  Limitations of isolating temperature alone are acknowledged later.  This constitutes a comprehensive explanation, though not exhaustively quantitative, hence a score of 5 within the 5â6 band.

The research question is clearly stated in the title block and again in bold within the text.  It is narrowly focused on the correlation between temperature (Â°C) and dissolved-oxygen concentration (mg Lâ»Â¹) in a controlled model of the Gulf of Mexico.  No ambiguity exists about variables, units or scope, and the time-frame and experimental context are embedded.  Although minor wording repetition appears, the question itself is coherent and sufficiently focused for data collection, justifying placement in the 5â6 band; the slightly informal phrasing ("to what extent") and modelling qualifier keep it at 5 rather than 6.

Strengths (broad environmental benefits) and weaknesses (economic difficulty, societal reliance on fossil fuels) of the proposed solution are mentioned, but evaluation is brief and lacks depth such as feasibility, cost or stakeholder analysis.  This corresponds to âevaluates some relevant strengths and weaknessesâ, i.e., band 2.

The report follows a logical section order (Introduction, Method, Data, Analysis, Evaluation, Application) and uses headings.  Some tables split awkwardly and images are poorly referenced, so organization is present but not sustained flawlessly, matching band 2.

Strengths (duration, native plants) and weaknesses (open system, light variability, single replicate) are identified and explained.  Some limitations such as lack of statistical replication and possible probe calibration drift are omitted, so the discussion is competent but not exhaustive, warranting 3 in the 3â4 band.

The student provides a multi-paragraph discussion of the northern Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone, citing surface area, key numerical DO thresholds and named authoritative sources (NOAA, EPA, National Geographic).  Causes (nutrient runoff, eutrophication, temperature) and ecological consequences are described and quantified, giving strong, relevant context.  Some statistics are generic and the economic dimension is absent, so the strand is well discussed but not exemplary, warranting 5 in the top band.

Sampling is implicitly one reading per tank per day; the student does not discuss the absence of replicates or explain why 20 days or single measurements are adequate.  Temperatures are justified, but that is a design choice rather than sampling rationale.  Consequently only an outline with clear omissions is provided, fitting the 1â2 band at the top of that range.

Electrical-shock risk, heavy-glass handling and ethical disposal of plants are noted, and mitigation is described (outlet distance, reuse of plants, sturdy platform).  However, wider ethical considerations (invasive-species risk, water disposal) and formal risk matrix are missing, so the description is competent but not comprehensive, meriting 3 in the 3â4 band.

All raw data are tabulated with units and uncertainty, and a single clearly labelled scatter graph with separate trendlines for each treatment displays every point.  Axes are correctly scaled, symbols differentiated and a 20-day span shown.  While additional statistical summaries could strengthen presentation, the chart nevertheless conveys all relevant data appropriately, satisfying the 5â6 band at level 5.

The student proposes regulating agricultural runoff and reducing GHG emissions, linking them generally to hypoxia and global warming.  Justification refers to the importance of temperature but does not explicitly use the numerical findings; nonetheless, some rationale is provided, meeting the descriptor for band 2.

Arguments generally flow, but some leaps (e.g., immediate policy prescriptions from aquarium data) and repeated statements disrupt coherence.  Minor formatting issues also interrupt reading.  The presentation remains mainly logical but difficult to follow at times, corresponding to band 2.

annotations.comment: The title effectively states a focused and coherent research question, clearly defining variables and context in the Gulf of Mexico model.
The phrasing âto what extentâ introduces informality and ambiguity. Consider rewording to âHow does water temperature affectâ¦â for precision.
The introduction concisely states the aim but merges methodology and broader importance. Separate the procedural steps from the environmental rationale to improve clarity.
Background effectively outlines the hypoxic zone, citing area, DO thresholds and authoritative sources, providing strong environmental context.
The sentence listing U.S. states ends abruptly. Ensure complete sentences to maintain professional tone and coherence.
The environmental issue discussion quantifies nutrient runoff and eutrophication impacts. For deeper context, include economic or social dimensions.
The hypothesis references a square-root correlation from another study but lacks a clear statement of expected mathematical relationship. Specify the predicted equation or correlation form.
The pilot study explanation clearly justifies switching to an immersion heater to avoid aeration bias, strengthening the method design.
Controlled variables table notes vase shape differences but does not address potential effects. Discuss how curved vs straight bases may alter oxygen diffusion.
Dependent variable description cuts off mid-sentence and omits calibration frequency. Complete the description to ensure repeatability.
The apparatus list is comprehensive and includes instrument uncertainties, supporting methodological transparency.
Ethical considerations mention plant reuse but omit water disposal protocols. Include environmental handling of experimental water to complete risk assessment.
The procedure figure lacks a clear caption or cross-reference in the text. Add labels and refer to it in the procedural description.
Procedure steps are explicit and sequential, facilitating reproducibility of measurements over 20 days.
Referring readers to the appendix for raw data is acceptable, but include a brief summary or sample in the results section for clarity.
No mention of replicate measurements or sample size beyond single readings. Incorporate replicates to strengthen statistical validity.
Figure 2 presents all data points and trendlines clearly with distinct symbols and labels.
The analysis notes negative correlation but omits RÂ² values here. Include quantitative metrics in the analysis narrative.
Attributing discrepancies solely to meter uncertainty overlooks biological demand. Discuss BOD effects or probe placement variability.
Identifying biological oxygen demand adds depth to the evaluation of methods and results.
The conclusion summarizes findings but lacks specific comparative rates of DO decline. Add numerical values to substantiate the claim.
Evaluation details experimental duration and plant selection strengths. To enhance, discuss the impact of uncontrolled sunlight variability quantitatively.
The discussion of openâsystem limitations provides a realistic assessment of model validity.
Reporting RÂ² values demonstrates awareness of statistical fit, but discuss what these values imply about model reliability.
Identifying model species-ratio mismatch is insightful. Next, propose how to adjust ratios quantitatively to better mimic the Gulf community.
Application recommendations are relevant but would benefit from linking specific DO decline metrics to policy thresholds.
The âConclusion:â header follows Application; consider relocating or renaming to avoid structural confusion.
The final conclusion ties results back to the real-world dead zone effectively, reinforcing the studyâs relevance.
Citation formatting varies across entries. Standardize to a single style (e.g., APA or MLA) for consistency.
The NOAA reference appears truncated without a complete title or URL. Provide full details to ensure traceability.
Long URLs break across lines, hindering readability. Adjust line breaks or hyperlink formatting.
